one_line_takeaway: Pretraining LLMs on deterministically-encrypted (AES-ECB) synthetic PII lets models learn domain knowledge and answer authorized encrypted queries while beating base models — but it is an exploratory, small-scale heuristic that explicitly trades cryptographic security (ECB leaks equality/frequency) for the determinism the model needs.
core_contribution: The paper proposes an exploratory framework for privacy-preserving continual pretraining of LLMs on encrypted synthetic data. Its own novel contributions are: (1) an entity-based, weighted-graph synthetic data generation pipeline that modifies EntiGraph (Yang et al., 2024) — combining LLM + NER + regex entity extraction (via Microsoft Presidio), scoring pairwise association strength to build a weighted entity graph, thresholding weak edges, and generating wider k-tuples (rather than EntiGraph's pairwise/3-tuples) to condition GPT-4o-mini/DeepSeek synthesis; and (2) applying deterministic symmetric encryption (AES in ECB mode, Base64-encoded with type prefixes like Person_[cipher]) to PII entities BEFORE synthesis, so the cloud provider never sees plaintext while the model learns entity-level associations over consistent ciphertext and authorized users recover answers via a decryption key. EntiGraph, homomorphic encryption (CipherGPT, CryptoNets, Rho et al.), MED, CipherDAug, TinyStories, and the Phi series are cited prior/alternative work, NOT this paper's contribution. The central design choice is a deliberate security-for-utility tradeoff: ECB is chosen precisely because it is deterministic (same plaintext → same ciphertext), which the authors need for the model to recognize repeated entities — at the explicit cost of cryptographic security.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious cloud provider that hosts continual pretraining and inference but must not access plaintext of the private corpus; the user holds the AES private key and encrypts queries / decrypts responses. Goal: sensitive PII stays hidden from the server and unauthorized users, while authorized key-holders retrieve plaintext answers. Residual leakage (stated by the authors): because AES-ECB is deterministic, an adversary with only the encrypted corpus and no key can still infer relationships between repeated entities and exploit equality/frequency statistics; ECB provides no semantic security and conceals no structural patterns, so the scheme offers no formal privacy guarantee. Additional leakage arises if encryption is applied after synthesis, where protection then depends on NER recall (rare/ambiguous entities may be missed).
limitations: Authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) AES-ECB is cryptographically insecure — deterministic ciphertext leaks equality/frequency patterns and has no semantic security; stronger deterministic schemes like AES-SIV are suggested but untested here. (2) Ciphertext replacing a PII entity spans >10 tokens, inflating sequence length and potentially harming long-context handling; representational/semantic effects of encryption on reasoning are not analyzed. (3) Method is validated only on small, domain-specific corpora (65K-token source, 197 total eval questions) where manual verification is feasible; scalability to large datasets is untested, and standard public benchmarks are inapplicable to encrypted data. (4) Reliance on LLMs for both extraction and synthesis risks fabricated/hallucinated content (~15% unique-entity hallucination observed). (5) No formal privacy proof — the mechanism is heuristic. Only two model sizes tested (LLaMA3-2B was near-random; 13B/70B not run due to compute).

datasets_used: QuALITY (English multiple-choice QA, 125 questions; PII-Quality subset of 35 name-containing questions)
Chinese Judge case dataset (China Judgements Online / Supreme People's Court, 72 questions, PII-annotated)
Synthetic corpus of 10M-30M tokens generated from a 65K-token source text
quantitative_results: Judge/Qwen2.5-7B: base 0.388, unencrypted synthetic 0.68, encrypted synthetic 0.611, encrypted-all-PII 0.569, orig 0.33, encO 0.40
QuALITY/Llama3-8B: base 0.424, synt 0.63, encS 0.592, orig 0.488, encO 0.424
PII masking: base and orig-pretrained accuracy collapse to 0.208; unmasked synthetic 0.68
noEncQ (crypto model on plaintext questions) ≈ base level, confirming no plaintext-ciphertext linkage learned
Ciphertext hallucination: 0.16 (names+locations) / 0.17 (all PII) of unique cipher entities; 0.029 / 0.039 of total ciphertext occurrences; only 5 nonexistent cipher entities
RAG improves accuracy: e.g. Judge encrypted top-4 retrieval 0.847 vs 0.388 no-retrieval; Quality top-16 unencrypted 0.672
baselines_compared: orig: continual pretraining on original (unencrypted) data
encO: continual pretraining on encrypted original data
noEncQ: crypto model evaluated with plaintext questions
